Title: Overexpression of improved EPSPS gene results in field level glyphosate tolerance and higher grain yield in rice

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher transgene expression under maize ubiquitin promoter than native OsEPSPS promoter; constitutive across tissues/developmental stages inferred from ZmUbi use
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ZmUbi constitutive promoter; native OsEPSPS promoter
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class I aroA / EPSPS enzyme with N-terminal chloroplast transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core chorismate/aromatic amino acid biosynthesis branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the enolpyruvyl moiety from phosphoenolpyruvate to shikimate-3-phosphate to form 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate in the shikimate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream, key control step in shikimate pathway preceding chorismate-derived aromatic amino acids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential aromatic amino acids for plant growth and development; precursors for many aromatic secondary metabolites and nutrients
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key enzyme and primary glyphosate target in the shikimate pathway; rate-limiting not directly proven
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glyphosate application in media, pots, greenhouse and field conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed mutagenesis; transgenic overexpression; PCR/Southern/northern/RT-PCR confirmation; seedling and field glyphosate assays; crop-weed competition assay; GC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Constitutive overexpression of glyphosate-insensitive mutant OsEPSPS confers strong field-level glyphosate tolerance, increases seed phenylalanine and tryptophan, and raises grain yield by 17–19% in the best lines
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Rice OsEPSPS carrying T173I+P177S (TIPS) and overexpressed from the ZmUbi promoter conferred high glyphosate tolerance up to 16.8 mM in field and 21 mM in pot conditions. 2. These lines showed no major fitness penalty and produced 17–19% higher grain yield than wild type without glyphosate. 3. Seeds accumulated ~6.5-fold more phenylalanine and ~2-fold more tryptophan than wild type. 4. Native promoter-driven expression was insufficient for strong field tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering glyphosate-tolerant, higher-yield rice; best deployed as TIPS-edited or transgenic OsEPSPS combined with a strong constitutive promoter; can be stacked with glyphosate detoxification genes and transgene-mitigation strategies
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Risk of transgene flow/fitness advantage to weedy rice; possible ecological selection for superweeds; no clear yield penalty observed in crop plants
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: GC-MS metabolite profiling; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper experimentally contrasts mutant forms rather than WT overexpression alone; double mutant outperformed triple mutant, indicating extra G172A mutation compromised performance

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highest in ZmUbi-driven DC lines; lower under native promoter DN lines
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ZmUbi constitutive promoter; native OsEPSPS promoter
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class I aroA / EPSPS enzyme with T173I and P177S substitutions; chloroplast transit peptide present
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core chorismate/aromatic amino acid biosynthesis branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes EPSP formation while the T173I and P177S substitutions reduce glyphosate sensitivity without abolishing catalytic function
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential amino acids and precursors for vitamins and aromatic secondary metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key glyphosate-targeted step; engineered branch-control point for aromatic amino acid supply
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glyphosate selection and spray treatments; greenhouse and field assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed mutagenesis; overexpression in rice; molecular confirmation; physiological glyphosate tolerance assays; field trials; GC-MS for amino acids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Best-performing engineered allele; markedly improves glyphosate tolerance and enhances grain yield plus phenylalanine/tryptophan accumulation when strongly overexpressed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. T173I+P177S mutant OsEPSPS under ZmUbi promoter provided robust glyphosate tolerance far superior to native-promoter lines. 2. DC1/DC2 lines survived herbicide doses lethal to wild type and controlled weeds in mixed culture. 3. These lines showed increased seeds per plant and 17–19% higher grain yield without glyphosate. 4. Seed phenylalanine and tryptophan increased substantially.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primary elite allele from this study for glyphosate-tolerant rice improvement; suitable for transgenic deployment or precise allele engineering if paired with high expression system
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential escape into weedy rice; fitness advantage could increase persistence if introgressed into weeds
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This was the main experimental gene in Results and is the strongest core pathway gene in the paper

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher under ZmUbi than native promoter, but phenotype weaker than TIPS lines
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ZmUbi constitutive promoter; native OsEPSPS promoter
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class I aroA / EPSPS enzyme with G172A, T173I and P177S substitutions; chloroplast transit peptide present
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core chorismate/aromatic amino acid biosynthesis branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes EPSP formation, but the added G172A substitution reduced the benefit of the TIPS glyphosate-tolerant configuration
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential aromatic amino acids and precursors of aromatic secondary metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key shikimate-pathway step; not the preferred engineered form
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glyphosate treatments in media and field comparisons
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed mutagenesis; overexpression in rice; molecular confirmation; glyphosate tolerance assays; GC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides only marginal glyphosate tolerance improvement and does not match TIPS-OsEPSPS performance, though amino acid increases were still observed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Triple-mutant G172A+T173I+P177S lines showed weaker glyphosate tolerance than TIPS lines. 2. Addition of G172A disrupted or reduced the tolerance benefit. 3. TC lines still showed elevated seed phenylalanine and some increase in tryptophan, but little agronomic gain versus wild type.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Less favorable than TIPS-OsEPSPS for engineering; useful negative-comparison allele showing that not all stacked mutations improve function
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced agronomic utility due to weaker tolerance; same potential transgene-flow concerns if deployed
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is a primary engineered EPSPS variant directly tested throughout the Results
